core_takeaway: Earlier position did not beat the last position here. Agent_1 anchored immediately on Pencil, but Pencil had zero value to Agents 2 and 3, while their own top bundles were also mutually compatible. The result was a unanimous allocation giving all three agents 100 utility, so position order did not create a measurable advantage.
why_or_counterexample: Counterexample to first-position advantage: the first speaker named the deal structure, but the game was noncompetitive at competition_level 0.0 and preferences were complementary. Agent_2 and Agent_3 accepted Pencil to Agent_1 because each could still receive their own 100-point bundle, and the final accepted proposal was submitted by Agent_3 rather than Agent_1.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by demanding Pencil, but Agent_2 and Agent_3 quickly aligned because the remaining items gave each of them their maximum payoff. The public negotiation became confirmation of a compatible three-way allocation rather than a contest over first-mover leverage.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private summaries confirm that all agents viewed the same coalition as payoff-maximizing or stable. Agent_1 planned to defend Pencil, while Agents 2 and 3 focused on preserving their own high-value bundles.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All three formal proposals were identical. This removes any proposal-order advantage: each agent proposed the same allocation, and the system ultimately recorded unanimous acceptance of Proposal #3.
phase_notes.private_voting: Every private vote accepted every proposal. Voting rationales emphasize immediate 100 utility, certainty, and identical proposals rather than deference to the first speaker.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection section is present in the transcript, matching reflection_entries=0.

mechanisms.explanation: The first agent's target item did not conflict with the later agents' top-valued bundles. Agent_2 could get Apple and Quill for 100, while Agent_3 could get Jewel and Stone for 100, so accepting Agent_1's Pencil demand did not cost them utility.

evidence.quote: Stone and Jewel are my top targets (88 + 12 = 100 total for me)

mechanisms.theme: Public anchoring became shared coalition framing
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 did introduce Pencil as the centerpiece, but the later agents independently reframed the proposal around their own compatible payoffs. Their confirmations show mutual fit rather than capitulation to an advantaged first mover.

evidence.quote: Pencil goes to me this round, and you two split the remaining four items
I secure my two highest-value items (Jewel + Stone).
I’m aligned with the proposed coalition and want to lock in this path.

mechanisms.theme: Identical formal proposals erase proposer advantage
mechanisms.explanation: Each agent submitted the same allocation. The formal proposal phase therefore does not show first-position dominance; Agent_3's proposal was the one marked accepted unanimously, even though all three proposals had the same content and vote totals.

mechanisms.theme: Private thinking shows each agent pursued maximum utility
mechanisms.explanation: Private thinking does not reveal later agents sacrificing for Agent_1. Agent_2 privately says the coalition yields its highest possible payoff, and Agent_3 privately says the coalition yields its highest personal payoff.

evidence.quote: Pencil is the non-negotiable centerpiece for me.
yields Agent_2 the highest possible payoff

mechanisms.theme: Private votes accepted because all agents got 100
mechanisms.explanation: The vote rationales are utility-based. Agent_1 accepted because Pencil gave 100 utility; Agent_2 accepted because Apple plus Quill gave 100; Agent_3 accepted because Jewel plus Stone gave 100. Equal final utilities match the row metadata first_minus_last=0.0.

evidence.quote: Pencil goes to Agent_1 (me) for 100 utility
Allocates Jewel+Stone to me (Agent_3) for 100 value

mechanisms.explanation: There is no reflection evidence to modify the interpretation. The review therefore relies on discussion, proposals, private thinking, and private votes.
